comments.text: If I unfolded the quantifiers correctly, I think at least one of #28 and #749 must be true: a counterexample to #28 generates an example to #749 for every epsilon.

I just want to check my understanding here: it seems to me that the hypothesis "all but finitely many" can be changed to "all"? That's because if $A+A$ contains all but finitely many [non-negative] integers, it seems we can enlarge $A$ by finitely many terms so that $A+A$ now contains literally all [non-negative] integers and in doing so we don't change whether $\limsup 1_A * 1_A(n)$ is finite. I was wondering whether someone could confirm this simple reasoning, because I feel that the modified conjecture sounds better.

Partially, yes. If we allow $0 \in A$ (as otherwise $1$ is not representable), then the conjectures are indeed equivalent. But to me the modified conjecture does not sound better, to be honest. When considering additive bases (e.g. Waring's problem or Goldbach's conjecture) one generally only cares about being able to represent large enough integers. Perhaps because of that, the assumption $\mathbb{N} \subseteq A + A$ feels at first glance unusually strong to me, weakening the (perceived) strength of the conjecture. Even though, as you note, they are actually equivalent. But as so often in maths; there's many ways to prove or state a result, and it sometimes just comes down to personal preference :) By the way, in this regard it is interesting to note that for the Erdős and Sárközy conjecture (Edit: which has since been moved to #1145) the equality $A + B = \mathbb{N}$ is written.

Thanks. In applications, and just as a mathematical statement, I definitely see the naturality of the sufficiently large assumption, but isolated by itself, I like the version without it. Nonetheless, it's notable that Goldbach's original conjecture was "*Every* positive even integer can be written as the sum of two primes", by including $1$ as a prime. And with Waring's problem, there are two versions: (following Wikipedia's notation) representing all integers is $g(k)$ while only sufficiently sufficiently large is $G(k)$. And we literally know only 3 total values of $G(k)$, as compared with $g(k)$ where it's known for almost all $k$! Re 0: maybe this page https://www.erdosproblems.com/definitions should specify whether $0\in\mathbb{N}$.

I'd rather leave it ambiguous whether $0\in \mathbb{N}$ - sometimes this makes sense, sometimes not. I think it's almost always obvious from the context whether $0$ is included or not (e.g. whether the statement is trivially true or false at $0$). If anyone knows of any places on this site where it actually makes a non-trivial difference I'd be interested to know.
